revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}]]|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōhiko no Mikoto}} [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Year of death unknown]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Generations of Jimmu that need correction]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[category:original pages]] ]] [[ja:大彦命]]

[[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}]]|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]

[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin]]}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}} [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Generations of Jimmu that need correction]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] ]] [[ja:大彦命]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}} [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Generations of Jimmu that need correction]] [[Category:original pages]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:ファイル]] ]] [[ja:大彦命]]

He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]

[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}} [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] ]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:大彦命]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}} [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] ]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:大彦命]]

[[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}} [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōhiko-no-Mikoto-qq]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōhiko-no-Mikoto-qq]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōhiko-no-Mikoto-qq]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōhiko-no-Mikoto-qq]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōhiko-no-Mikoto-qq]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōhiko-no-Mikoto-qq]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Ōhiko-no-Mikoto-qq]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] ]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:大彦命]]

{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - {{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}} [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}}[[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:11]] [[ja:大彦命]]

The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also! == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}} [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}}[[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:11]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[ja:大彦命]]

{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko no Mikoto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}

[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
