He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]]]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] {{citation needed|Ohiko no Mikoto}} {{citation needed|Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]]]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] {{citation needed|Ohiko no Mikoto}} {{citation needed|Ohiko-no-Mikoto}}

revision.comment: 102 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:大彦命 up to rev 101653484
revision.text:
{{特殊文字|説明=[[Microsoftコードページ932]]（[[はしご高]]）など}} [[File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|thumb|220px|[[歌川国芳]]『日本百将伝』より大彦命の想像図]] '''大彦命'''（おおひこのみこと{{Sfn|大彦命（国史）}}/おおびこのみこと{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}、生没年不詳）は、[[記紀]]等に伝わる古代[[日本]]の[[皇族]]。 『[[日本書紀]]』では「大彦命」、『[[古事記]]』では「'''大毘古命'''」と表記される。また[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]の銘文に見える「'''意富比垝'''」に比定する説がある。 第8代[[孝元天皇]]の第1[[皇子]]で、第11代[[垂仁天皇]]の[[外祖父]]である。また、[[阿倍氏|阿倍臣（阿倍氏）]]を始めとする諸氏族の祖。[[四道将軍]]の1人で、[[北陸道|北陸]]に派遣されたという。 == 系譜 ==
'''『[[日本書紀]]』に基づく関係系図''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[孝霊天皇]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[孝元天皇]]|02=[[吉備津彦命]]
［西道］|03=[[倭迹迹日百襲媛命|{{color|#FC4E6B|倭迹迹日百襲媛命}}]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[開化天皇]]|02='''大彦命'''
［北陸］|03=[[武埴安彦命]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[彦坐王|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[崇神天皇]]|03=[[御間城姫|{{color|#FC4E6B|御間城姫}}]]|04=[[武渟川別]]
［東海］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[丹波道主命]]
［丹波］|02={{Supra|11}} [[垂仁天皇]]}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|（名称は『日本書紀』を第一とし、括弧内に『古事記』ほかを記載）}} 第8代[[孝元天皇]]と[[欝色雄命]]の妹で[[皇后]]の[[欝色謎命]]（うつしこめのみこと、内色許売命）との間に生まれた第1[[皇子]]である。同母兄弟として[[開化天皇]]（第9代）、[[少彦男心命]]（すくなひこをこころのみこと、少名日子建猪心命<すくなひこたけゐこころのみこと>）、[[倭迹迹姫命]]（やまとととひめのみこと、古事記なし）がいる。 子として『[[日本書紀]]』では[[御間城姫]]（みまきひめ、御真津比売命：第10代[[崇神天皇]]皇后）、[[武渟川別]]（たけぬなかわわけ、建沼河別命）の名が、『[[古事記]]』では加えて[[比古伊那許志別命]]（ひこいなごしわけのみこと）の名が見える。御間城姫は[[垂仁天皇]]（第11代）の生母であり、大彦命はその[[外祖父]]になる。 == 記録 == === 文献 === 『[[日本書紀]]』[[崇神天皇]]10年9月9日条では大彦命を[[北陸道|北陸]]に派遣するとあり、同書では[[東海道|東海]]に派遣される[[武渟川別]]、[[山陽道|西道]]に派遣される[[吉備津彦命]]、[[丹波国|丹波]]に派遣される[[丹波道主命]]とともに「[[四道将軍]]」と総称されている{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 同書崇神天皇9月27日条では、大彦命はその途中の和珥坂（または山背の平坂）で不吉な歌を詠う少女に会ったため、引き返して天皇にこのことを報告した。そして[[倭迹迹日百襲媛命]]（[[孝霊天皇]][[皇女]]で、大彦命のおば）の占いによって[[武埴安彦命]]（大彦命の異母兄弟）とその妻の吾田媛の謀反が発覚する。果たして実際に謀反が起こると、五十狭芹彦命（吉備津彦命）が吾田媛を、大彦命と[[彦国葺命|彦国葺]]（[[和珥氏|和珥臣]]祖）が共に武埴安彦を討ち鎮圧した{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。その後、四道将軍らは崇神天皇10年10月22日に出発し、崇神天皇11年4月28日に平定を報告したという。 『[[古事記]]』では、建波邇安王（武埴安彦命）の鎮圧においては同様の説話を記す。一方、四道将軍としての4人の派遣ではないが、やはり崇神天皇の時に大毘古命（大彦命）は高志道に、建沼河別命は東方十二道に派遣されたとする。そして大毘古命と建沼河別命が出会った地が「相津」（現・[[福島県]][[会津]]）と名付けられた、と地名起源説話を伝える。 なお『[[新撰姓氏録]]』河内国皇別難波忌寸条では、崇神天皇の時に大彦命が蝦夷平定に向かった際、大彦命は兎田墨坂（うだのすみさか：現・[[奈良県]][[宇陀市]]榛原萩原）で嬰児を拾って育て、「得彦（えひこ）宿禰」と名付けたと伝える{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 ==== 伝承 ==== [[葛木坐火雷神社]]に伝わる旧記によれば、[[武埴安彦命]]討伐の際に、[[天火明命]]の末裔の笛吹連櫂子を率いたという[http://fuefukijinja.org/yuisyo.html] - 葛木坐火雷神社（笛吹神社）公式ウェブサイト。 === 考古資料 === {{Double image aside|right|稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣 (表面).JPG|100|稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣 (裏面).JPG|100|{{Center|[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]（国宝）}}{{small|[[埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館]]展示。左は表面、右は裏面。}}}} [[埼玉県]][[行田市]]の[[埼玉古墳群]]にある[[稲荷山古墳 (行田市)|稲荷山古墳]]で出土した鉄剣（[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]）には銘文があり、その銘文に見える人物の「'''意富比垝'''（おほひこ）」を大彦命に比定する説が挙げられている{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。銘文記載の系譜は次の通り。
{{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0|01|-|02|-|03|-|04|.| 01=意富比垝|02=多加利足尼|03=弖已加利獲居|04=多加披次獲居}} {{familytree|border=0||||,|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|'}} {{familytree|border=0||||`|01|-|02|-|03|-|04| 01=多沙鬼獲居|02=半弖比|03=加差披余|04=乎獲居臣}} {{familytree/end}}
{{-}} == 後裔 == === 人物 === * 子 ** [[武渟川別]]（建沼河別命） - 四道将軍の1人で、東海に派遣。『古事記』では[[安部氏|阿倍臣]]らの祖。 ** [[御間城姫]]（御真津比売命） - 第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇后]]で、第11代[[垂仁天皇]]生母。 ** 比古伊那許志別命（彦背立大稲腰命/彦瀬立大稲起命）『古事記』孝元天皇記、『新撰姓氏録』左京[[皇別]] [[完人氏|完人]][[朝臣]]条等。 - 『古事記』では[[膳氏|膳臣]]の祖。 ** 波多武彦命（波多武日子命）?『新撰姓氏録』[[摂津国]][[皇別]] 摂津国皇別 三宅人条では「大彦命男」、[[河内国]]皇別 難波条では「大彦命孫」と記載。 ** 紐結命（比毛由比命）『新撰姓氏録』河内国皇別 日下連条等。 * 孫 ** [[磐鹿六雁命]]（伊波我牟都加利命）『新撰姓氏録』左京皇別 膳大伴部条。 ** 彦屋主田心命『新撰姓氏録』右京皇別 [[道氏|道公]]条等。 ** 波多武彦命（波多武日子命）? * 五世孫 ** 田道命『先代旧事本紀』「国造本紀」[[筑紫国造|筑志国造]]条。 * 八世孫 ** 稚子臣『新撰姓氏録』左京皇別 [[阿倍志斐氏|阿倍志斐連]]条。 === 氏族 === 『日本書紀』では[[阿倍氏|阿倍臣]]を始め、[[膳氏|膳臣]]（かしわでのおみ、後の髙橋臣）、[[阿閉氏|阿閉臣]]（あへのおみ、阿敢臣）、[[沙沙貴山君|狭狭城山君]]、[[筑紫国造]]、[[越国造]]、伊賀臣ら7氏の始祖とする。ただし『古事記』では、建沼河別命（[[武渟川別]]）を阿倍臣等の祖、比古伊那許志別命を膳臣の祖に位置づけている。 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』では、次の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 左京[[皇別]] - [[阿倍氏|阿倍]][[朝臣]]、[[布勢氏|布勢朝臣]]、[[完人氏|完人朝臣]]、高橋朝臣、[[許曽倍氏|許曽倍朝臣]]、[[阿閉氏|阿閉臣]]、[[竹田氏|竹田臣]]、[[名張氏|名張臣]]、[[沙沙貴山君|佐々貴山公]]、膳大伴部、阿倍志斐連 * 右京皇別 - [[若桜部氏|若桜部朝臣]]、阿閉臣、伊賀臣、[[阿閉間人氏|阿閉間人臣]]、[[他田広瀬氏|他田広瀬朝臣]]、[[道氏|道公]]、音太部、会加臣、杖部造 * [[山城国]]皇別 - 阿閇臣 * [[大和国]]皇別 - 坂合部首 * [[摂津国]]皇別 - 高橋朝臣、[[沙沙貴山君|佐々貴山君]]、[[久々智氏|久々智]]、坂合部、伊我水取、吉志、三宅人 * [[河内国]]皇別 - 阿閇朝臣、阿閇臣、日下連、大戸首、難波[[忌寸]]、難波 * [[和泉国]]皇別 - 膳臣、[[宇太氏|宇太臣]]、[[松原氏|松原臣]]、他田 == 信仰 == *[[古四王神社]]（[[秋田県]][[秋田市]]） - 北陸地方には「古四王」「高志王」等として「越王」すなわち大彦命を祀る神社が分布する。 * [[伊佐須美神社]]（[[福島県]][[大沼郡]][[会津美里町]]） - 会津での大彦命と武渟川別との出会いに起源を持つ。 * [[鵜坂神社]]（[[富山県]][[富山市]]） * [[舟津神社]]（[[福井県]][[鯖江市]]） * [[敢國神社]]（[[三重県]][[伊賀市]]） - 阿倍枝族の阿閉臣（阿敢臣）分布地。同神社より北東1.5Km程の所に位置する御墓山古墳は社伝によると大彦命の御陵とされている。 * [[布勢神社 (さぬき市)|布勢神社]]（[[香川県]][[さぬき市]]） == 考証 == [[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]に見える「意富比垝」を大彦命に比定する説では、乎獲居臣を大彦命の後裔を称する阿倍氏か膳氏の一族ではないか、と見る解釈が多いが、その後の時代、北[[武蔵国|武蔵]]で大きな勢力を築いていた壬生吉士（難波吉士と同族）の可能性もあるとされる。[[首都大学東京]]教授の[[川口勝康]]は「稲荷山古墳出土の鉄剣銘文中の乎獲居臣なる人物の系譜にみえる上祖の意富比垝は、オホヒコとよまれ、記紀の大彦命にあたる可能性が高い」としつつも、その実在性には慎重で、当時著名であった大彦命の東征説話を知った乎獲居臣がその祖を大彦命に求めたものであろうと解説し『世界大百科事典』（[[平凡社]]、2007年版）。、[[京都大学]]名誉教授の[[岸俊男]]は「ヲワケを東国国造の系譜に属する者と考える説と、上祖オホヒコを記紀に阿倍臣や膳臣の始祖としてみえる孝元天皇の皇子大彦命とし、あるいは杖刀人は阿倍臣に従属する丈部（はせつかべ）であるとみて、ヲワケを中央豪族の一員と考える説に大きく見解が分かれている」と解説する。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''原典''' {{reflist|group="原"}} '''出典''' {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=上田正昭|authorlink=上田正昭|year=|chapter=大彦命|title=国史大辞典|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|大彦命（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=大彦命|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=9784642014588|ref={{Harvid|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=青垣出版|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[四道将軍]] {{DEFAULTSORT:おおひこのみこと}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:日本古代の武人・武官]] [[Category:阿倍氏]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[大彦命]] to [[Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{特殊文字|説明=[[Microsoftコードページ932]]（[[はしご高]]）など}} [[File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|thumb|220px|[[歌川国芳]]『日本百将伝』より大彦命の想像図]] '''大彦命'''（おおひこのみこと{{Sfn|大彦命（国史）}}/おおびこのみこと{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}、生没年不詳）は、[[記紀]]等に伝わる古代[[日本]]の[[皇族]]。 『[[日本書紀]]』では「大彦命」、『[[古事記]]』では「'''大毘古命'''」と表記される。また[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]の銘文に見える「'''意富比垝'''」に比定する説がある。 第8代[[孝元天皇]]の第1[[皇子]]で、第11代[[垂仁天皇]]の[[外祖父]]である。また、[[阿倍氏|阿倍臣（阿倍氏）]]を始めとする諸氏族の祖。[[四道将軍]]の1人で、[[北陸道|北陸]]に派遣されたという。 == 系譜 ==
'''『[[日本書紀]]』に基づく関係系図''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[孝霊天皇]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[孝元天皇]]|02=[[吉備津彦命]]
［西道］|03=[[倭迹迹日百襲媛命|{{color|#FC4E6B|倭迹迹日百襲媛命}}]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[開化天皇]]|02='''大彦命'''
［北陸］|03=[[武埴安彦命]]}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[彦坐王|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[崇神天皇]]|03=[[御間城姫|{{color|#FC4E6B|御間城姫}}]]|04=[[武渟川別]]
［東海］}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[丹波道主命]]
［丹波］|02={{Supra|11}} [[垂仁天皇]]}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|（名称は『日本書紀』を第一とし、括弧内に『古事記』ほかを記載）}} 第8代[[孝元天皇]]と[[欝色雄命]]の妹で[[皇后]]の[[欝色謎命]]（うつしこめのみこと、内色許売命）との間に生まれた第1[[皇子]]である。同母兄弟として[[開化天皇]]（第9代）、[[少彦男心命]]（すくなひこをこころのみこと、少名日子建猪心命<すくなひこたけゐこころのみこと>）、[[倭迹迹姫命]]（やまとととひめのみこと、古事記なし）がいる。 子として『[[日本書紀]]』では[[御間城姫]]（みまきひめ、御真津比売命：第10代[[崇神天皇]]皇后）、[[武渟川別]]（たけぬなかわわけ、建沼河別命）の名が、『[[古事記]]』では加えて[[比古伊那許志別命]]（ひこいなごしわけのみこと）の名が見える。御間城姫は[[垂仁天皇]]（第11代）の生母であり、大彦命はその[[外祖父]]になる。 == 記録 == === 文献 === 『[[日本書紀]]』[[崇神天皇]]10年9月9日条では大彦命を[[北陸道|北陸]]に派遣するとあり、同書では[[東海道|東海]]に派遣される[[武渟川別]]、[[山陽道|西道]]に派遣される[[吉備津彦命]]、[[丹波国|丹波]]に派遣される[[丹波道主命]]とともに「[[四道将軍]]」と総称されている{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 同書崇神天皇9月27日条では、大彦命はその途中の和珥坂（または山背の平坂）で不吉な歌を詠う少女に会ったため、引き返して天皇にこのことを報告した。そして[[倭迹迹日百襲媛命]]（[[孝霊天皇]][[皇女]]で、大彦命のおば）の占いによって[[武埴安彦命]]（大彦命の異母兄弟）とその妻の吾田媛の謀反が発覚する。果たして実際に謀反が起こると、五十狭芹彦命（吉備津彦命）が吾田媛を、大彦命と[[彦国葺命|彦国葺]]（[[和珥氏|和珥臣]]祖）が共に武埴安彦を討ち鎮圧した{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。その後、四道将軍らは崇神天皇10年10月22日に出発し、崇神天皇11年4月28日に平定を報告したという。 『[[古事記]]』では、建波邇安王（武埴安彦命）の鎮圧においては同様の説話を記す。一方、四道将軍としての4人の派遣ではないが、やはり崇神天皇の時に大毘古命（大彦命）は高志道に、建沼河別命は東方十二道に派遣されたとする。そして大毘古命と建沼河別命が出会った地が「相津」（現・[[福島県]][[会津]]）と名付けられた、と地名起源説話を伝える。 なお『[[新撰姓氏録]]』河内国皇別難波忌寸条では、崇神天皇の時に大彦命が蝦夷平定に向かった際、大彦命は兎田墨坂（うだのすみさか：現・[[奈良県]][[宇陀市]]榛原萩原）で嬰児を拾って育て、「得彦（えひこ）宿禰」と名付けたと伝える{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 ==== 伝承 ==== [[葛木坐火雷神社]]に伝わる旧記によれば、[[武埴安彦命]]討伐の際に、[[天火明命]]の末裔の笛吹連櫂子を率いたという[http://fuefukijinja.org/yuisyo.html] - 葛木坐火雷神社（笛吹神社）公式ウェブサイト。 === 考古資料 === {{Double image aside|right|稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣 (表面).JPG|100|稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣 (裏面).JPG|100|{{Center|[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]（国宝）}}{{small|[[埼玉県立さきたま史跡の博物館]]展示。左は表面、右は裏面。}}}} [[埼玉県]][[行田市]]の[[埼玉古墳群]]にある[[稲荷山古墳 (行田市)|稲荷山古墳]]で出土した鉄剣（[[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]）には銘文があり、その銘文に見える人物の「'''意富比垝'''（おほひこ）」を大彦命に比定する説が挙げられている{{Sfn|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。銘文記載の系譜は次の通り。
{{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0|01|-|02|-|03|-|04|.| 01=意富比垝|02=多加利足尼|03=弖已加利獲居|04=多加披次獲居}} {{familytree|border=0||||,|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|-|'}} {{familytree|border=0||||`|01|-|02|-|03|-|04| 01=多沙鬼獲居|02=半弖比|03=加差披余|04=乎獲居臣}} {{familytree/end}}
{{-}} == 後裔 == === 人物 === * 子 ** [[武渟川別]]（建沼河別命） - 四道将軍の1人で、東海に派遣。『古事記』では[[安部氏|阿倍臣]]らの祖。 ** [[御間城姫]]（御真津比売命） - 第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇后]]で、第11代[[垂仁天皇]]生母。 ** 比古伊那許志別命（彦背立大稲腰命/彦瀬立大稲起命）『古事記』孝元天皇記、『新撰姓氏録』左京[[皇別]] [[完人氏|完人]][[朝臣]]条等。 - 『古事記』では[[膳氏|膳臣]]の祖。 ** 波多武彦命（波多武日子命）?『新撰姓氏録』[[摂津国]][[皇別]] 摂津国皇別 三宅人条では「大彦命男」、[[河内国]]皇別 難波条では「大彦命孫」と記載。 ** 紐結命（比毛由比命）『新撰姓氏録』河内国皇別 日下連条等。 * 孫 ** [[磐鹿六雁命]]（伊波我牟都加利命）『新撰姓氏録』左京皇別 膳大伴部条。 ** 彦屋主田心命『新撰姓氏録』右京皇別 [[道氏|道公]]条等。 ** 波多武彦命（波多武日子命）? * 五世孫 ** 田道命『先代旧事本紀』「国造本紀」[[筑紫国造|筑志国造]]条。 * 八世孫 ** 稚子臣『新撰姓氏録』左京皇別 [[阿倍志斐氏|阿倍志斐連]]条。 === 氏族 === 『日本書紀』では[[阿倍氏|阿倍臣]]を始め、[[膳氏|膳臣]]（かしわでのおみ、後の髙橋臣）、[[阿閉氏|阿閉臣]]（あへのおみ、阿敢臣）、[[沙沙貴山君|狭狭城山君]]、[[筑紫国造]]、[[越国造]]、伊賀臣ら7氏の始祖とする。ただし『古事記』では、建沼河別命（[[武渟川別]]）を阿倍臣等の祖、比古伊那許志別命を膳臣の祖に位置づけている。 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』では、次の氏族が後裔として記載されている。 * 左京[[皇別]] - [[阿倍氏|阿倍]][[朝臣]]、[[布勢氏|布勢朝臣]]、[[完人氏|完人朝臣]]、高橋朝臣、[[許曽倍氏|許曽倍朝臣]]、[[阿閉氏|阿閉臣]]、[[竹田氏|竹田臣]]、[[名張氏|名張臣]]、[[沙沙貴山君|佐々貴山公]]、膳大伴部、阿倍志斐連 * 右京皇別 - [[若桜部氏|若桜部朝臣]]、阿閉臣、伊賀臣、[[阿閉間人氏|阿閉間人臣]]、[[他田広瀬氏|他田広瀬朝臣]]、[[道氏|道公]]、音太部、会加臣、杖部造 * [[山城国]]皇別 - 阿閇臣 * [[大和国]]皇別 - 坂合部首 * [[摂津国]]皇別 - 高橋朝臣、[[沙沙貴山君|佐々貴山君]]、[[久々智氏|久々智]]、坂合部、伊我水取、吉志、三宅人 * [[河内国]]皇別 - 阿閇朝臣、阿閇臣、日下連、大戸首、難波[[忌寸]]、難波 * [[和泉国]]皇別 - 膳臣、[[宇太氏|宇太臣]]、[[松原氏|松原臣]]、他田 == 信仰 == *[[古四王神社]]（[[秋田県]][[秋田市]]） - 北陸地方には「古四王」「高志王」等として「越王」すなわち大彦命を祀る神社が分布する。 * [[伊佐須美神社]]（[[福島県]][[大沼郡]][[会津美里町]]） - 会津での大彦命と武渟川別との出会いに起源を持つ。 * [[鵜坂神社]]（[[富山県]][[富山市]]） * [[舟津神社]]（[[福井県]][[鯖江市]]） * [[敢國神社]]（[[三重県]][[伊賀市]]） - 阿倍枝族の阿閉臣（阿敢臣）分布地。同神社より北東1.5Km程の所に位置する御墓山古墳は社伝によると大彦命の御陵とされている。 * [[布勢神社 (さぬき市)|布勢神社]]（[[香川県]][[さぬき市]]） == 考証 == [[稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣]]に見える「意富比垝」を大彦命に比定する説では、乎獲居臣を大彦命の後裔を称する阿倍氏か膳氏の一族ではないか、と見る解釈が多いが、その後の時代、北[[武蔵国|武蔵]]で大きな勢力を築いていた壬生吉士（難波吉士と同族）の可能性もあるとされる。[[首都大学東京]]教授の[[川口勝康]]は「稲荷山古墳出土の鉄剣銘文中の乎獲居臣なる人物の系譜にみえる上祖の意富比垝は、オホヒコとよまれ、記紀の大彦命にあたる可能性が高い」としつつも、その実在性には慎重で、当時著名であった大彦命の東征説話を知った乎獲居臣がその祖を大彦命に求めたものであろうと解説し『世界大百科事典』（[[平凡社]]、2007年版）。、[[京都大学]]名誉教授の[[岸俊男]]は「ヲワケを東国国造の系譜に属する者と考える説と、上祖オホヒコを記紀に阿倍臣や膳臣の始祖としてみえる孝元天皇の皇子大彦命とし、あるいは杖刀人は阿倍臣に従属する丈部（はせつかべ）であるとみて、ヲワケを中央豪族の一員と考える説に大きく見解が分かれている」と解説する。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''原典''' {{reflist|group="原"}} '''出典''' {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=上田正昭|authorlink=上田正昭|year=|chapter=大彦命|title=国史大辞典|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|大彦命（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=大彦命|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=9784642014588|ref={{Harvid|大彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=宝賀寿男|authorlink=宝賀寿男|year=2013|chapter=|title=阿倍氏 四道将軍の後裔たち|publisher=青垣出版|isbn=978-4434176753|ref={{Harvid|宝賀|2013年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[四道将軍]] {{DEFAULTSORT:おおひこのみこと}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:日本古代の武人・武官]] [[Category:阿倍氏]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

revision.comment: Bot: restore translated version
revision.text:
{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]]]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] {{citation needed|Ohiko no Mikoto}} {{citation needed|Ohiko-no-Mikoto}} {{translated page|ja|大彦命|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}}

He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== ]] [[Category:translated pages without meta categories]]\n{{translated page|ja|大彦命|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]]

{{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko no Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko no Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko no Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Four Commanders of the Four Roads|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko no Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Mikoto|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko no Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko no Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== ]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] }} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]]

His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== ]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]]

The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== ]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]]

He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==

{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== ]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

[[Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== ]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Shidō Shōgun]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:生没年不詳]] [[Category:阿倍氏]] [[zh:大彦命]]

{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== ]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:生没年不詳]] [[Category:阿倍氏]] [[zh:大彦命]]

He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== ]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year of Birth and Death unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:大彦命]]

He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]

[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:大彦命]]

{{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|qid=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|qid=Q11071228}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|qid=Q11546050}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|qid=Q333572}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|qid=Q11544859}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|qid=Q333548}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|qid=Q11487911}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|qid=Q11545865}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|qid=Q11387096}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11435420]] Wikidata name: Prince Ōhiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funatsu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:大彦命]]

{{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|qid=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|qid=Q11071228}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|qid=Q11546050}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|qid=Q333572}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|qid=Q11544859}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|qid=Q333548}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|qid=Q11487911}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|qid=Q11545865}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|qid=Q11387096}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11435420]] Wikidata name: Prince Ōhiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:大彦命]]

{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] }} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[Category:Qq 4]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}}

[[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11435420]] Wikidata name: Prince Ōhiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
