{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - revision.text:
[[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11435420]] Wikidata name: Prince Ōhiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}}

[[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11435420]] Wikidata name: Prince Ōhiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:大彦命]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}}

[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[zh:大彦命]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}}

The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11435420]] Wikidata name: Prince Ōhiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]], Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]] and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]] and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the [[:en:Hokurikudō|Hokurikudō]] (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the [[:en:Inariyama Kofun|Inariyama Kofun]] is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11435420]] Wikidata name: Prince Ōhiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen}}, Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen}}|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika}}|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen}} and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika}} and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō}} (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun}} is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

revision.text:
[[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096}}}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen}}, Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen}}|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika}}|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen}} and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika}} and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}, and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō}} (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun}} is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]][[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[sn:Ōhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

revision.text:
[[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252|1=Yamato Kingship}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228|1=Mimaki-hime}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050|1=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|1=Emperor Kōgen}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859|1=Utsushikome}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|1=Emperor Kaika}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911|1=Hikofutsuoshi no Makoto no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865|1=Prince Takehaniyasuhiko}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096|1=Yamatototohime no mikoto}}}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}}, Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}}|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika|1=Emperor Kaika}}|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}} and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika|1=Emperor Kaika}} and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō|1=Hokurikudō}} (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun}} is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
[[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252|1=Yamato Kingship}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228|1=Mimaki-hime}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050|1=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|1=Emperor Kōgen}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859|1=Utsushikome}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|1=Emperor Kaika}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911|1=Hikofutsuoshi no Makoto no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865|1=Prince Takehaniyasuhiko}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096|1=Yamatototohime no mikoto}}}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}}, Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}}|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika|1=Emperor Kaika}}|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}} and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika|1=Emperor Kaika}} and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō|1=Hokurikudō}} (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun}} is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

[[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]]}} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTŌhiko-no-Mikoto]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]

revision.text:
[[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] [[en:Prince Ōhiko]][[ja:大彦命]][[zh:大彦命]] {{Infobox person|image=File:大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252|1=Yamato Kingship}}|children={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228|1=Mimaki-hime}}; {{ill|Takenunakawawake|WD=Q11546050|1=Take Nunokawa Wake no Mikoto}}|father={{ill|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|1=Emperor Kōgen}}|mother={{ill|Empress Utsusikome|WD=Q11544859|1=Utsushikome}}|relatives={{ill|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|1=Emperor Kaika}}; {{ill|Prince Hikofutsuoshinomakoto|WD=Q11487911|1=Hikofutsuoshi no Makoto no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Prince Takehaniyasuhiko|WD=Q11545865|1=Prince Takehaniyasuhiko}}; {{ill|Yamatototohime no mikoto|WD=Q11387096|1=Yamatototohime no mikoto}}}} {{Infobox Royalty | name = Ōhiko-no-Mikoto | native_name = 大彦命 | other_name = Ōbiko no Mikoto (大毘古命) | image = 大彦命 Ōhiko no mikoto 日本百将伝.jpg | caption = Depiction of Ōhiko-no-Mikoto from "Japanese Hundred Generals Biography" by Utagawa Kuniyoshi | era = [[Ancient Japan]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | burial_place = Possibly [[Mimuroyama Kofun]] | spouse = | children = [[Mimaki-hime]] (Empress of the 10th Emperor [[Suinin]]), [[Takenunakawawake]] (武渟川別), [[Hiko Inakoshiwake no Mikoto]] (比古伊那許志別命, ''Kojiki'' only) | parents = Father: {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}}, Mother: [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] | role = [[Shidō Shōgun]] (General of the Four Routes) }} '''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto''' (大彦命/おおひこのみこと, dates unknown) was a legendary prince and warrior of ancient Japan recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''[[Kojiki]]''. He is one of the [[Shidō Shōgun|Shidō Shōgun]] (Generals of the Four Routes) and an ancestor of many clans, including the powerful [[Abe Clan]]. == Genealogy ==
'''Genealogy Based on the ''Nihon Shoki''''' {{familytree/start|style="text-align:center; font-size:100%"}} {{familytree|border=0||||01| 01={{Supra|7}} [[Emperor Kōrei}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|8}} {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}}|02=[[Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|Kibitsuhiko no Mikoto]]
[[Western Route]]|03=[[Yamatototohimemiko|{{color|#FC4E6B|Yamatototohimemiko}}}} {{familytree|border=0|||||)|-|-|-|-|v|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0||||01|||02|03| 01={{Supra|9}} {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika|1=Emperor Kaika}}|02='''Ōhiko-no-Mikoto'''
[[:en:Northern Route|Northern Route]]|03=[[Takehaniyasuhiko}} {{familytree|border=0|||,|-|(|||||)|-|.|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|02|y|~|03|04| 01=[[Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|○]]|02={{Supra|10}} {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}|03=[[Mimaki-hime|{{color|#FC4E6B|Mimaki-hime}}|04=[[Takenunakawawake]]
[[Eastern Route}} {{familytree|border=0|||!||||!|}} {{familytree|border=0|01|||02| 01=[[Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto|Tanba Michinushi no Mikoto]]
[[Tanba Route]]|02={{Supra|11}} [[Emperor Suinin}} {{familytree/end}}
{{Smaller|(Names prioritize the ''Nihon Shoki,'' with alternatives from ''Kojiki'' in parentheses)}} Ōhiko-no-Mikoto was the eldest prince of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}} and [[Utsushikome no Mikoto]] (also known as Utsushikome, 内色許売命). His siblings included {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika|1=Emperor Kaika}} and [[Yamatototohimemiko]]. He fathered [[Mimaki-hime]], the empress of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, and [[Takenunakawawake]], another Shidō Shōgun. == Historical Role == === Chronicles === The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions Ōhiko as one of the four generals dispatched by {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} to pacify Japan. He was assigned to the {{ill|Hokurikudō|en|Hokurikudō|WD=Q3139239|lt=Hokurikudō|1=Hokurikudō}} (Northern Route), while other generals took the Western, Eastern, and Tanba Routes. During a reconnaissance mission, Ōhiko encountered a singing girl who foretold rebellion. He reported this to Emperor Sujin, leading to the discovery of a conspiracy by [[Takehaniyasuhiko]] and his wife. Ōhiko and his allies defeated the rebels, solidifying their role in early Japanese governance. The ''[[Kojiki]]'' similarly narrates his role in the suppression of Takehaniyasuhiko and records his dispatch to the Koshi region. === Inscriptions === The [[Inariyama Sword Inscription]] found in the {{ill|Inariyama Kofun|en|Inariyama Kofun|WD=Q3198272|lt=Inariyama Kofun|1=Inariyama Kofun}} is believed to reference Ōhiko as "Ōhiko" (意富比垝). The inscription lists several generations of rulers linked to Ōhiko's lineage, suggesting his role as a foundational figure. == Descendants == Ōhiko-no-Mikoto is regarded as the progenitor of numerous clans: - [[Abe Clan]] - [[Kashiwade Clan]] (later [[Takahashi Clan]]) - [[Ahe Clan]] - [[Sasaki Clan]] - [[Tsukushi Province Clan]] - [[Koshi Province Clan]] - [[Iga Province Clan]] The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' mentions his descendants in several regional aristocratic families. == Worship == Ōhiko is enshrined as a deity in several shrines, particularly in the Hokuriku region: - [[Koshi Shrine]] (Akita Prefecture) - [[Isasumi Shrine]] (Fukushima Prefecture) - [[Usaka Shrine]] (Toyama Prefecture) - [[Funazu Shrine]] (Fukui Prefecture) - [[Kokoku Shrine]] (Mie Prefecture) == References == '''Primary Sources''' {{reflist|group="Primary"}} '''Secondary Sources''' {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans|author=Ueda Masao|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|year=2010}} * {{Cite book|title=Abe Clan and the Four Generals of the Routes|author=Hoga Hisao|publisher=Aogaki Publishing|year=2013}} == See Also == - [[Shidō Shōgun]] ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu}} ==Footnotes== ===Notes=== ===References=== {{translated page|ja|大彦命|qq=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|version=101653484|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[simple:Prince Ōhiko]] [[ja:大彦命]] [[zh:大彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11435420}} [[Category:Abe clan]] [[Category:Abe Clan]] [[Category:Japanese warriors]] [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year of death unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]
